chunk_summary: This section focuses on evaluating dietary diversity and meal frequency indicators for children aged 6–23 months, using UNICEF and WHO guidelines, alongside insights from NFHS-5 data and socioeconomic studies in Delhi.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of dietary diversity and meal frequency in assessing child nutrition.
Learn how UNICEF and WHO indicators are applied to evaluate complementary feeding practices.
Analyze data from NFHS-5 and socioeconomic studies to identify disparities in feeding practices.
Explore strategies to improve minimum acceptable diet adherence in children aged 6–23 months.
key_facts: Minimum dietary diversity (MDD) is defined as consuming foods from at least 5 of 8 food groups daily for children aged 6–23 months (UNICEF).
Minimum meal frequency (MMF) varies by age and breastfeeding status, with 2–4 meals per day recommended for children aged 6–23 months (UNICEF).
Minimum acceptable diet (MAD) combines MDD and MMF to ensure adequate nutrition (UNICEF).
NFHS-5 (2019–21) data highlights disparities in dietary practices across socioeconomic groups in India.
WHO 2021 indicators provide a standardized framework for assessing complementary feeding practices globally.
reference_quote: Minimum dietary diversity (MDD) is achieved when children aged 6–23 months consume foods from at least five out of eight food groups daily. (UNICEF Data Indicator Profile)
document_key_topics: Dietary diversity indicators for children aged 6–23 months
Global and India-specific feeding practices data
